rationale_for_baseline_tier: A vulnerable young adult with Down syndrome has acutely decompensated over 12 hours with objective hypoxia (SpO2 92% on room air), tachypnoea (RR 28) with accessory-muscle use, fever, a purulent cough he is too weak to clear, and a severe drop in conscious level with inability to drink. This warrants urgent in-person emergency assessment now — oxygen assessment, chest imaging, bloods, sepsis screening and consideration of intravenous therapy and admission. Although the in-person clinician elected supervised home management, that decision rested on auscultation localising a focal treatable pneumonia, exclusion of a new cardiac murmur despite the repaired AVSD, and arranged in-person nursing — all unavailable to a remote clinician. Telehealth cannot exclude sepsis, respiratory failure or a cardiac contribution here, so the safe disposition uplifts to T4, with an immediate T5/000 pathway on any deterioration trigger. He is bedbound, hypoxic and drowsy, so ambulance transport is appropriate rather than self-transport.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Blue lips or fingers, breathing rate over 30 per minute, or becoming unrousable or confused
escalation_edges.rationale: These are the note's own emergency thresholds and signal impending respiratory failure or critical deterioration requiring an emergency ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Call 000 for an ambulance straight away if his lips or fingers turn blue, if his breathing rate goes over 30 breaths a minute, or if he cannot be woken properly or seems confused.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Home oxygen saturation staying at or dropping below 92%, or breathing getting harder
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Do not wait — call 000 / get him to emergency care now
escalation_edges.rationale: Persistent or falling hypoxia in a tiring, hypotonic patient indicates worsening oxygenation failure that needs supplemental oxygen and monitored transport.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the oximeter stays at 92% or drops lower, or his breathing gets harder, call 000 now — he needs oxygen and emergency assessment.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Continued inability to take any fluids, or signs of dehydration (very dry mouth, not passing urine)
escalation_edges.rationale: Ongoing poor intake compounds circulatory compromise and reduces the safety of any home-based plan overnight.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he still cannot take any fluids, he needs to be seen urgently today — do not wait until the morning.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Down syndrome with generalised hypotonia (weak cough) and a repaired atrioventricular septal defect
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for in-person review: immune vulnerability and impaired secretion clearance drive rapid decompensation, and the cardiac history keeps a cardiac contribution in play that cannot be excluded remotely.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Support-worker-mediated history; the patient is normally verbal but currently too drowsy to self-report
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Because he cannot describe his own symptoms or deterioration, weight the carer's observations and objective home readings heavily and lower the threshold for escalation — a communication barrier is an escalation reason, not a reassurance.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Arrange urgent in-person emergency assessment for him now — given he is bedbound, drowsy and his oxygen is low, calling an ambulance for monitored transport is appropriate. While waiting, keep him sitting fully upright, keep monitoring his breathing rate and the home oximeter, and do not give anything that makes him drowsy. Call 000 immediately if his lips or fingers turn blue, his breathing rate goes over 30, he cannot be woken or becomes confused, or his oxygen level falls further.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: 000 for an emergency ambulance / urgent emergency department assessment
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 and the emergency department are available 24 hours — this cannot wait for clinic hours or for tomorrow's planned reviews.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the group-home observation chart (temperatures, breathing rates, oximeter readings, oral intake), his Down syndrome and repaired-AVSD history, and the antibiotic and antipyretic already given, to the emergency team and GP.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Reading this as a routine chest infection or attributing the deterioration to his disability, and advising home management, GP tomorrow or reassurance.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A hypoxic, septic-risk vulnerable adult deteriorates overnight in the group home without the oxygen, imaging and monitored care he needed, progressing to respiratory failure.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognising urgency but directing only to same-day urgent care rather than emergency assessment, under-providing for hypoxia and possible sepsis while the patient continues to tire.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Defaulting straight to a 000 ambulance before any deterioration trigger is present is defensible here given hypoxia and drowsiness; an ambulance for a hypoxic bedbound vulnerable adult is a reasonable use of the emergency pathway and is not penalised as harmful over-triage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether this is a focal treatable pneumonia safe for supervised home antibiotics (as the in-person clinician judged) or an evolving sepsis / respiratory failure — and whether the repaired AVSD is contributing — cannot be resolved remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the unexcludable serious course as present and escalate for urgent in-person assessment; provide concrete deterioration triggers and 000 advice.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Assuming the benign end of the range (routine chest infection, manage at home) because a single home oximeter reading is '92%' and the patient 'always looks like this'.
